body_txt: A trio of beautiful women: one with brown or black hair, one with blonde, one with red. The hair equivalent to chocolate, vanilla, and strawberry. The trope is strictly for visual appeal; they are not personality types . Sometimes, they're heroes. Sometimes, they're friends. Sometimes, they're contenders , fighting for the same love interest. Whatever the details, they're proof that Central Casting does count : three women, three natural hair colors. Collect the full set . This trope was inspired—or at least popularlized—by The Andrews Sisters , a Girl Group from the 1940s who were famous for entertaining US army forces during World War Two . Their diversity of hair colors—all natural, no less—was due to their mixed Norwegian-Greek ancestry. For the purposes of this trope, the hair can be a wig or dye job, especially if the women aren't all white. But it must still be three women with these three colors to qualify. Simply having a gaggle of women with different hair colors—especially colors beyond the natural spectrum—is not a trope. And although this trope is traditionally used with women, it can certainly be invoked with men or mixed genders, though in such cases, the masculinized spellings "blond" and "brunet" are applicable instead of "blonde" and "brunette". See also Chromatic Arrangement ; Power Trio ; The Three Faces of Eve ; The Hecate Sisters ; Beauty, Brains, and Brawn .
